# Discussion

## Synthesis of Evidence: The BCMA Revolution

This systematic review demonstrates that BCMA-directed therapies have fundamentally transformed the treatment landscape for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (R/R MM). The evidence, drawn from pivotal registration trials, long-term follow-up analyses, and emerging real-world data, consistently indicates that both CAR T-cell products and bispecific antibodies targeting BCMA achieve unprecedented response rates in heavily pretreated patients, including those with triple-class refractory disease. Idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel) yielded an overall response rate of 73% in KarMMa [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene], while ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel) achieved 98% in CARTITUDE-1 with deep and durable responses at two-year follow-up [@Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Martin2022Ciltacabtagene]. The randomized phase 3 KarMMa-3 and CARTITUDE-4 trials subsequently confirmed the superiority of these agents over standard-of-care regimens in earlier lines of relapse, with significant improvements in progression-free survival [@RodriguezOtero2023Idecel; @SanMiguel2023Ciltacel]. These findings collectively establish BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapy as a new standard of care for patients who have exhausted conventional options.

Bispecific antibodies represent a complementary modality within the BCMA-directed armamentarium. Teclistamab demonstrated an overall response rate of 63% in the MajesTEC-1 trial among patients with a median of five prior lines of therapy [@Moreau2022Teclistamab], while elranatamab achieved 61% in MagnetisMM-3 [@Lesokhin2023Elranatamab]. Although cross-trial comparisons must be interpreted cautiously, the slightly lower response rates observed with bispecific antibodies relative to CAR T-cell products may reflect differences in patient populations, depth of prior treatment exposure, and mechanistic distinctions in how these agents engage T cells. The ability of bispecific antibodies to be administered off-the-shelf without the manufacturing delays inherent to autologous CAR T-cell production represents a substantial practical advantage, particularly for patients with rapidly progressive disease who cannot afford the four-to-six-week manufacturing interval [@Cappell2023Longterm].

## Treatment Sequencing Challenges

The proliferation of effective agents across multiple drug classes has created complex sequencing dilemmas. The conventional paradigm of PI-IMiD-anti-CD38 antibody combinations in early lines followed by BCMA-directed therapy at later relapse is increasingly being challenged by data supporting earlier deployment of immunotherapy [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel; @RodriguezOtero2023Idecel]. The CARTITUDE-4 trial demonstrated that cilta-cel significantly improved progression-free survival compared with physician's choice in lenalidomide-refractory patients after one to three prior lines of therapy, raising the question of whether delaying BCMA-directed therapy until patients are heavily pretreated and immunologically compromised may represent a missed opportunity [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel].

However, several factors complicate earlier use of these therapies. The finite duration of CAR T-cell responses, even in patients achieving deep remissions, means that retreatment strategies must be considered [@Cappell2023Longterm]. Resistance mechanisms, including biallelic loss of BCMA, antigen downregulation, and T-cell exhaustion, have been documented and may limit the efficacy of subsequent BCMA-directed approaches [@Samur2021Biallelic]. The availability of non-BCMA targets such as GPRC5D, as exemplified by talquetamab, provides an alternative pathway for patients who progress after BCMA-directed therapy, though the optimal sequencing of these agents relative to BCMA-targeted treatments remains undefined [@Chari2022Talquetamab].

## CAR T-Cell Therapy Versus Bispecific Antibodies

The comparison between CAR T-cell therapy and bispecific antibodies represents one of the most clinically relevant questions in R/R MM management. Both modalities redirect autologous T cells to target myeloma cells but differ fundamentally in their delivery, kinetics, and toxicity profiles. CAR T-cell therapy offers the advantage of a one-time infusion with the potential for prolonged antitumor activity mediated by in vivo expansion and persistence of engineered T cells [@Raje2019AntiBCMA; @Munshi2021Idecabtagene]. In contrast, bispecific antibodies require continuous or repeated dosing but provide immediate availability and the capacity for dose modification in response to adverse events [@Moreau2022Teclistamab].

From a toxicity standpoint, cytokine release syndrome (CRS) occurs with both modalities but tends to be more severe with CAR T-cell therapy. Neurotoxicity, including the distinctive movement and neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNT) observed with cilta-cel, represents a unique concern for certain CAR T-cell products [@Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene]. Bispecific antibodies are associated with high rates of infection, partly attributable to sustained hypogammaglobulinemia and T-cell dysfunction resulting from ongoing target engagement [@Moreau2022Teclistamab; @Lesokhin2023Elranatamab]. Head-to-head trials comparing these modalities are not yet available, and treatment selection currently depends on clinical factors including disease kinetics, patient fitness, logistical considerations, and institutional capabilities.

## Access, Cost, and Health Equity

The transformative efficacy of BCMA-directed therapies is tempered by significant barriers to access. CAR T-cell manufacturing requires specialized infrastructure, REMS-certified treatment centers, and substantial financial resources, with costs exceeding several hundred thousand dollars per patient in the United States. Real-world data indicate that healthcare resource utilization varies substantially between inpatient and outpatient CAR T-cell delivery settings, with implications for both costs and clinical outcomes [@Janakiram2026Realworld]. Bispecific antibodies, while more readily available, require indefinite dosing with associated cumulative costs and the burden of frequent clinic visits. Geographic disparities in access to both modalities disproportionately affect patients in low- and middle-income countries, where anti-CD38 antibodies may themselves be inaccessible, let alone next-generation immunotherapies [@van2021Multiple; @Padala2021Epidemiology].

## Infection Risk with T-Cell Redirecting Therapies

A critical and underappreciated consequence of T-cell redirecting therapies is the heightened susceptibility to infections. Both CAR T-cell products and bispecific antibodies deplete normal B-cell and plasma cell populations, resulting in prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia and impaired humoral immunity [@Cappell2023Longterm]. Infections represent a leading cause of non-relapse mortality following BCMA-directed therapy, with opportunistic infections including Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, cytomegalovirus reactivation, and invasive fungal disease reported across clinical trials. The infectious risk is compounded in heavily pretreated patients whose immune systems are already compromised by prior therapies, including high-dose alkylators and prolonged corticosteroid exposure [@Rajkumar2022Multiple]. Standardized prophylactic strategies, including immunoglobulin replacement, antimicrobial prophylaxis, and vaccination protocols, are evolving but lack robust evidence from randomized trials.

## Strengths and Limitations

This review has several strengths. It provides a comprehensive synthesis of the evidence base spanning proteasome inhibitors, immunomodulatory drugs, anti-CD38 antibodies, BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapies, bispecific antibodies, and emerging non-BCMA targets. The inclusion of both randomized controlled trials and real-world evidence offers a balanced perspective on efficacy and effectiveness. Systematic search strategies across multiple databases minimize the risk of omitting relevant studies.

Several limitations warrant acknowledgment. First, publication bias may have led to the over-representation of positive results, particularly for newer agents where industry-sponsored trials predominate. Negative or equivocal studies may remain unpublished or published only as conference abstracts, introducing reporting bias into the evidence base. Second, the certainty of evidence varies substantially across therapeutic domains. While BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapies are supported by randomized phase 3 data, many bispecific antibody and non-BCMA target studies remain at the single-arm phase 1/2 level, limiting the ability to draw definitive comparative conclusions. Formal GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluations) assessment of the evidence was not performed for all outcomes, which limits the ability to assign standardized certainty ratings across the full scope of this review. The rationale for this decision reflects the heterogeneity of study designs and endpoints across the included literature. Third, the rapidly evolving landscape means that studies published after our search date may alter the conclusions drawn here, representing an inherent gap in any systematic review of an active field. Fourth, cross-trial comparisons are limited by differences in patient populations, eligibility criteria, response assessment methods, and follow-up duration.

## Future Directions

Several promising avenues are poised to reshape the R/R MM treatment paradigm. The movement toward earlier deployment of immunotherapy, supported by the CARTITUDE-4 and KarMMa-3 results, is likely to accelerate, with ongoing trials evaluating BCMA-directed agents in newly diagnosed high-risk patients [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel; @RodriguezOtero2023Idecel]. Combination strategies pairing bispecific antibodies with IMiDs, anti-CD38 antibodies, or other bispecific antibodies targeting different antigens represent a logical evolution aimed at deepening responses and overcoming resistance mechanisms.

The development of allogeneic or off-the-shelf CAR T-cell products addresses the critical limitation of manufacturing time and could democratize access to cellular therapy [@Maalej2023CARcell]. Gene-editing technologies, including CRISPR-Cas9-mediated approaches, offer the potential for universal donor cells with reduced alloreactivity. Simultaneously, a deeper understanding of resistance mechanisms---including antigen loss, T-cell exhaustion, and microenvironmental immunosuppression---will inform the design of next-generation therapies [@Samur2021Biallelic; @Wang2022Therapeutic]. The integration of MRD-guided strategies with functional immune profiling may ultimately enable precision sequencing of therapies, moving the field toward an individualized treatment paradigm that maximizes duration of disease control while minimizing cumulative toxicity [@Rajkumar2022Multiple].

# Conclusion

This systematic review synthesizes the current evidence on therapeutic strategies for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma, a disease that despite remarkable advances remains incurable for the majority of patients. The past decade has witnessed a paradigm shift from reliance on proteasome inhibitors, immunomodulatory drugs, and anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies as the primary therapeutic backbone to the incorporation of BCMA-directed immunotherapies that achieve response rates previously unattainable in heavily pretreated populations [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene; @Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Moreau2022Teclistamab].

The evidence reviewed here supports several key conclusions. First, BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapies---ide-cel and cilta-cel---represent the most efficacious agents available for triple-class refractory myeloma, with randomized data now confirming benefit in earlier relapse settings [@RodriguezOtero2023Idecel; @SanMiguel2023Ciltacel]. Second, bispecific antibodies targeting BCMA and GPRC5D provide critical off-the-shelf alternatives that expand treatment options, though their long-term efficacy and infectious complications require ongoing scrutiny [@Moreau2022Teclistamab; @Chari2022Talquetamab; @Lesokhin2023Elranatamab]. Third, treatment sequencing remains an unresolved challenge, with the optimal integration of novel immunotherapies into the broader treatment algorithm requiring prospective evaluation.

Critical gaps persist. Access to BCMA-directed therapies remains inequitable, with cost, infrastructure, and geographic barriers limiting their reach. The infectious complications of T-cell redirecting therapies demand better prophylactic and supportive care frameworks. Resistance mechanisms, including antigen loss and T-cell exhaustion, necessitate continued investment in next-generation platforms such as allogeneic CAR T cells, multi-targeted approaches, and rationally designed combination regimens [@Samur2021Biallelic; @Maalej2023CARcell]. As the therapeutic armamentarium continues to expand, the integration of MRD-guided strategies, real-world evidence, and patient-centered outcomes will be essential for translating the promise of these innovations into sustained improvements in survival and quality of life for all patients with R/R MM.
